document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance Children's Health Insurance Program Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance Children's Health Insurance Program Guide
root.summary: Business-level guide to Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance CHIP. Explains the program purpose and audience, then outlines CHIP as a state/federal children’s coverage program within the Medical Assistance cascading eligibility hierarchy. Key eligibility facts include: children under 19, uninsured, no other comprehensive coverage, open enrollment limits with exceptions (e.g. newborns), 90-day uninsured rule, and financial eligibility based on income only up to 200% FPL with no resource test. Covers household/financial unit and benefit-group rules, premium/copay assessment, managed care vs fee-for-service context, and creation of CHIP product delivery with 12-month certification. Also describes reassessment and change-of-circumstance processing, including income changes, adding/removing members, newborn handling, and end-date/disenrollment rules. The node also contains front matter notes, contents, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the Notices section before using the information or product. It also shows the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory and eligibility overview for the Cúram CHIP guide. Covers document purpose/audience, CHIP background, and how CHIP fits into cascading Medical Assistance eligibility. Key operational points: CHIP is for uninsured children under 19, open enrollment applies with exceptions, premium support may be state-controlled, and reassessment is included. Eligibility requirements include nonfinancial criteria, no other comprehensive insurance (with HIPAA-defined exceptions and good-cause waivers), 90-day uninsured rule, and financial rules: income-based only, no resource test, financial unit/household size definitions, and income limit up to 200% FPL.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-22
children.summary: Explains CHIP household composition and optional assistance units, where eligible household members are grouped and selected into separate product deliveries. Covers creating CHIP product delivery, premium/copayment calculation, effective date and 12-month certification, and benefit group rules. Also describes reassessment on ongoing eligibility and change of circumstance, including income changes, member add/remove, and cases that terminate coverage. Useful for understanding CHIP enrollment, coverage maintenance, and caseworker actions in Cúram.

Overview of the Cúram CHIP guide for Medical Assistance. Explains CHIP purpose, audience, and how CHIP eligibility fits the cascading Medical Assistance hierarchy. Covers core rules: uninsured children under 19, open enrollment with exceptions, premiums/copays, no other comprehensive insurance, 90-day uninsured rule, and income-only financial eligibility up to 200% FPL with no resource test. Also describes household composition/benefit groups, CHIP product delivery creation, 12-month certification, and reassessment for ongoing eligibility and changes in circumstance, including income, enrollment, and member add/remove actions.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This page contains legal notices: sample data disclaimer, copyright license for sample programs, warranty/liability limitations, and a privacy policy reference linking to Merative’s website. It also lists trademarks and copyright ownership for Merative and third-party brands.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that sample names and data are fictitious, and that sample source programs may be copied, modified, and distributed under the Merative Client Agreement for developing conforming applications, but are provided “AS IS” without warranty. Includes Merative privacy policy link. Lists trademark notices for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, plus a general notice that other names may be trademarks.

Legal notice for the publication. It sets terms for personal and enterprise use, requiring preservation of proprietary notices and prohibiting derivative or external distribution without consent. It disclaims warranties, states the content is provided “AS IS,” reserves patent rights, notes export-law compliance, and says non-Merative product info and external websites are not endorsed and are used at the reader’s risk. Includes Merative copyright notice.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance Children’s Health Insurance Program Guide. Lists front matter (Note, Edition) and major chapters: introduction, CHIP overview, eligibility determination and product delivery case creation, and reassessment. Also includes subtopics such as open enrollment rules, non-financial and financial eligibility requirements, household/income rules, eligibility result, and product delivery home page. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance Children's Health Insurance Program Guide
1.1 Introduction
Document Purpose
children.summary: This section explains the document’s purpose and audience for the Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance CHIP guide. It gives a business-level overview of CHIP implementation, not detailed application instructions. It is intended for business users who want to understand CHIP, the evidence captured, eligibility checking, and creation/management of CHIP Product Delivery. It also points readers to related Medical Assistance documentation, including the main program guide and guides for Long Term Care and Spend Down.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business users seeking a business-level understanding of Cúram’s CHIP implementation. It explains the program’s purpose, the evidence recorded in the system, how eligibility is checked, and how CHIP Product Delivery is created and managed. The section also points readers to related Medical Assistance documentation, including the broader Income Support for Medical Assistance guide and related Long Term Care and Spend Down guides.

children.title: Available Documentation
children.summary: Lists related documentation for Cúram Medical Assistance programs. It points readers to the Business Analysts guide, Income Support for Medical Assistance Program, as the main supporting document for Medical Assistance and CHIP, and also references related guides for Long Term Care and Spend Down. Useful if you need companion documentation or want to follow links to broader program guides before reading the CHIP overview.

Introduces the CHIP guide and its purpose: a business-level overview of Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance CHIP, with limited application specifics. It targets business users and explains the program, captured evidence, eligibility checking, and creation/management of CHIP Product Delivery. It also points to related documentation, especially the Income Support for Medical Assistance Program guide and guides for Long Term Care and Spend Down.

children.title: 1.2 Children's Health Insurance Program Overview
About Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP)
children.summary: Explains what CHIP is and how Cúram handles it. Covers CHIP’s U.S. origins, joint federal/state funding, state flexibility, covered services, premiums and co-payments, and managed care vs fee-for-service delivery. Also describes Cúram’s CHIP as part of Medical Assistance for eligible children under 19, using evidence and cascading eligibility rules. Notes CHIP is evaluated only after categorically and medically needy coverage types, and the priority hierarchy is configurable by state.

children.title: The Cúram Children's Health Insurance Program
Eligibility Determination and Product Delivery Case Creation
children.summary: This section explains CHIP eligibility and product delivery creation. It notes that CHIP applications are accepted only during open enrollment periods, tracked by an Enrollment Details processing entity, and may depend on state premium requirements managed via an environment variable and external vendor. It covers how eligibility rules identify possible assistance units, how families choose which children to cover, and how caseworkers check CHIP eligibility alone or with all Medical Assistance programs. It also introduces reassessment for ongoing eligibility or changes in circumstances.

children.title: Reassessment
children.summary: Explains CHIP reassessment for an existing product delivery. Reassessment occurs during ongoing eligibility or after a change in circumstances. The surrounding CHIP guide also notes open enrollment restrictions, optional participation by eligible families, possible premiums controlled by state-specific settings, and external vendor handling of premium billing. If you need the process for reevaluating CHIP coverage or understanding how CHIP fits into broader medical assistance eligibility and case creation, open this node.

Introduces CHIP in Cúram as a state-administered, jointly funded program for children under 19 who lack insurance and are ineligible for Medical Assistance. Explains that CHIP eligibility is determined through evidence-based business rules within the Medical Assistance cascading eligibility hierarchy: CHIP is considered only after categorically needy and medically needy coverage types are ruled out. Notes that this hierarchy is configurable by state to meet local program requirements.

Overview of CHIP in Cúram: a state-administered, jointly funded U.S. program for children under 19 whose families earn too much for Medical Assistance but cannot afford private insurance. Describes federal/state roles, state flexibility in benefits, eligibility, premiums/co-payments, and managed care vs fee-for-service delivery. In Cúram, CHIP is part of Medical Assistance and uses evidence-based, cascading eligibility rules; CHIP is evaluated only after categorically needy and medically needy coverage types, with the priority hierarchy configurable by state.

children.title: 1.3 Eligibility Determination and Product Delivery Case Creation
Introduction
children.summary: Introduction to CHIP in Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance. Explains that CHIP applications are only accepted during open enrollment periods due to limited funding and slots, with Enrollment Details recording accepted periods. Notes that CHIP is optional for eligible individuals, has no mandatory assistance unit members, may require state-controlled premiums, and premium billing is handled by an external vendor. Also previews eligibility determination, product delivery case creation, and reassessment, including the ability to check CHIP alone or as part of all Medical Assistance programs.

children.title: Check CHIP Eligibility
children.summary: This section explains how CHIP eligibility is checked within Medical Assistance eligibility. It notes that CHIP is included in the broader eligibility hierarchy, and that caseworkers can run checks for either all Medical Assistance programs or CHIP only from application or integrated case home pages. It also highlights CHIP-specific operational constraints: open enrollment periods, state-controlled enrollment limits, optional participation, possible premiums, and external vendor handling of premium billing and collections.

children.title: Rules
children.summary: This section outlines CHIP eligibility rules. It explains that CHIP is only evaluated after Medical Assistance coverage types, and a child must satisfy open enrollment, nonfinancial requirements, CHIP-specific eligibility, household financial rules, and household composition rules. It describes open enrollment periods and exceptions allowing some children to enroll outside the period, such as newborn/adopted children or children losing other Medical Assistance coverage. It also states the nonfinancial requirements: citizenship, SSN, and residency.

children.title: Open Enrollment Rules
children.summary: Explains CHIP open enrollment rules within the eligibility sequence. CHIP applications are accepted only during organization-defined open enrollment periods, subject to funding limits. Exceptions allow enrollment without waiting for the next period if: a child already enrolled in CHIP has a new birth/adoption in the household; an additional child loses Medical Assistance coverage and is ineligible for other MA without spend down; or a child loses MA coverage and has no other MA eligibility without spend down. Also notes CHIP is checked only after higher-priority MA coverage types.

children.title: Non Financial Requirements
children.summary: This section explains CHIP eligibility rules, focusing on non-financial requirements. A child must meet Citizenship, SSN, and Residency standards, along with open enrollment, CHIP-specific, financial, and household composition rules. It also notes CHIP’s relationship to Medical Assistance eligibility and that higher-priority coverage types may be determined first in the eligibility hierarchy.

children.title: Eligibility Requirements
children.summary: Lists CHIP eligibility rules for children: under 19, not otherwise eligible for Medicaid/Medical Assistance, no other comprehensive health insurance, uninsured for 90 days unless Good Cause applies, not in certain institutions or inpatient psychiatric care, and not recently disenrolled for nonpayment. Defines “comprehensive coverage” (e.g., group/private/employer plans, Medicare, CHAMPUS/CHAMPVA, public health plans) and notes exceptions for employer-plan waiting periods and Good Cause loss of insurance reasons.

children.title: Comprehensive Health Insurance
children.summary: Defines CHIP “comprehensive health insurance” and related eligibility rules. Lists coverage types that count as comprehensive, including group/employer plans, Medicare, CHAMPUS/CHAMPVA, public health plans, Peace Corps coverage, private policies, and government-employee family coverage. Notes that a child is considered insured if entitled to employer/group coverage costing under 10% of household gross income, unless a waiting period applies. Also explains CHIP may cover children during employer-plan waiting periods and gives “good cause” reasons that waive the 90-day uninsured requirement.

children.title: Good Cause for Loss of Health Insurance reasons
children.summary: Lists “good cause” exceptions to CHIP’s 90-day uninsured waiting period. No waiting period applies if health insurance ended because the covered person died, the policyholder lost coverage due to job loss/layoff/reduction-in-force/business closure, or the employer stopped contributing to family coverage. Use this section to determine when a child may qualify for CHIP immediately after losing prior insurance.

children.title: Financial Eligibility Requirements
children.summary: Explains CHIP financial eligibility rules. A household must pass an income test but not a resource test. CHIP uses its own rules for financial unit and household size; the financial unit includes the eligible child, certain siblings, parents/step-parents, one non-parent caretaker relative, children of counted children, spouses, and unborn children, but excludes SSI recipients. Household size equals the financial unit. Only countable income is used, and adjusted gross countable income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.

children.title: Financial Unit
Financial Unit Exception
children.summary: This section defines CHIP financial eligibility basics: the household must pass an income test but no resource test, and CHIP uses its own rules for financial unit and household size. It explains who is included in the financial unit, with the key exception that SSI recipients are excluded. It also notes that household size matches the financial unit and that adjusted gross countable income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.

Defines CHIP financial eligibility basics: the household must pass an income test but not a resource test. Explains the financial unit used for counting income—child, siblings under 19, parents/step-parents, one caretaker relative, children of counted children, spouses of counted children, and unborn children—with SSI recipients excluded. Household size equals the financial unit. Countable income rules apply, and adjusted gross countable income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.

children.title: Household Size
children.summary: Explains CHIP financial eligibility rules for household size. The household size equals the financial unit, which includes the eligible child, certain siblings under 19, parents/step-parents, one non-parent caretaker relative in some cases, children of counted children, spouses of counted children, and unborn children. SSI recipients are excluded from the financial unit. Eligibility uses income rules only, no resource test, and adjusted gross countable income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level for the household size.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: Explains CHIP financial eligibility and income rules. Describes when prior insurance loss can qualify a child, notes no resource test is required, and defines the financial unit/household size used for eligibility. The financial unit includes the child, certain siblings, parents/step-parents, one non-parent caretaker relative, children of counted children, spouses, and unborn children, but excludes SSI recipients. Income eligibility is based on countable income only, and adjusted gross countable income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.

children.title: Household Composition Rules
children.summary: Explains CHIP household composition rules for forming assistance units. Unlike LIFC, eligible CHIP household members are optional and may be split into different benefit groups. Uses an example household to show multiple possible assistance-unit combinations, but in the results page these are consolidated into one selectable result per product delivery. Caseworkers choose which members proceed, and separate product deliveries are created when members cannot be in the same benefit group. Also states that a child may be grouped with eligible siblings, half, adopted, and step-siblings.

children.title: Eligibility Result
children.summary: This section explains the eligibility result shown after determination, listing Medical Assistance coverage types (including CHIP) for which household members are eligible, along with ineligible decisions and reasons. It also describes how a household member chooses a coverage type to apply for. For CHIP, it covers creating CHIP product delivery: selecting assistance unit members, forming CHIP cases, calculating premiums and co-payment limits from rate tables, and repeating the process for each selected CHIP program before product delivery creation.

children.title: Create CHIP Product Delivery
Case Creation
children.summary: Explains CHIP case creation behavior: premium and copayment records are created when premiums apply, with premium data stored on a linked premium entity and billing handled by an external vendor. The effective coverage date is set at case creation using the application date, possibly adjusted by a grace period, and certification is auto-set to 12 months from that date. Also covers the product delivery home page links for premium details and copayment tracking, and notes that certifications are view-only for caseworkers.

children.title: Effective Date Of Coverage
children.summary: Explains how CHIP coverage is started and managed in Cúram. Coverage begins on the application date, or earlier by a granted grace period when special circumstances prevent timely filing, and is set at case creation along with the certification period. Also notes premium creation and external billing, co-payment record handling and limits, product delivery page links, and that CHIP certifications are system-controlled with a fixed 12-month period and view-only caseworker access.

Explains how CHIP product delivery is created after eligibility results: eligible household members are grouped into assistance units, a CHIP case is created per unit, and members are selected to confirm who will proceed. Premiums and co-payment maximum limits are calculated using household size, income, children applying, and rate tables. Premium and co-payment details are reviewed and accepted before product delivery is created for each selected CHIP program. The section highlights CHIP-specific processing within standard product delivery creation.

children.title: Product Delivery Home Page
Premiums
children.summary: Explains CHIP premium handling: when premiums are required, the calculated amount and frequency are stored on the premium entity, with co-payment records also created; billing/collection/allocation are done by an external vendor. Notes CHIP coverage effective date is the application date (or earlier with a grace period) and certification is system-controlled. The product delivery home page adds links to view premium details and record/view co-payments. Premiums are required only if an environment variable is set and families are not exempt.

children.title: Co-Payments
children.summary: Explains CHIP co-payment handling in Cúram. A copayment record is created at case creation with the household limit and coverage period. To track when the maximum co-payment limit is reached, receipt details must be recorded; the limit-reached indicator is set when total receipts for the case meet or exceed the limit. The section also notes co-payments are accessed from the product delivery home page alongside premium details.

children.title: Certifications
children.summary: Explains CHIP certification handling in Cúram. Certification is system-controlled and based on the effective date, with the certification period set to 12 months from that date and not editable by caseworkers. The Certifications page is view-only for CHIP; add and modify certification functions were removed. This section also sits alongside CHIP case creation details such as effective date of coverage, premiums, and co-payments.

children.title: Benefit Group
children.summary: Explains the Benefit Group concept for medical assistance coverage, especially CHIP. The benefit group is the household members eligible for a coverage type, initially derived from eligibility results and for CHIP can be modified by a caseworker. CHIP Benefit Group pages let workers add/remove household members already on the integrated case if they were most recently deemed CHIP-eligible. Also introduces CHIP reassessment: ongoing eligibility checks, premium/payment requirements, state-configurable frequency, cancellation and waiting periods, and possible case closure after two missed months.

This section describes the CHIP product delivery home page and related case setup. It explains that premium amounts and frequency are stored on the premium entity, co-payment records are created at case creation, and billing/collection are handled by an external vendor. It also covers CHIP coverage effective date and certification rules: both are system-set at case creation, with certification fixed at 12 months and view-only. The home page adds links for premium details and recording/viewing co-payments.

Explains CHIP eligibility determination and product delivery creation. CHIP is checked within the Medical Assistance hierarchy only after higher-priority coverage types, and can be run for CHIP only or all MA programs. Covers open enrollment limits and exceptions, nonfinancial rules, CHIP-specific eligibility, financial unit/household size and 200% FPL income limit, and optional household composition/assistance-unit grouping. Also describes eligibility results, selecting members, creating CHIP cases/product deliveries, premiums and copay calculations, external vendor billing, coverage effective date, and 12-month system-set certification.

children.title: 1.4 Reassessment
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces CHIP benefit groups and reassessment. Benefit groups are the household members eligible for a coverage type; CHIP benefit groups are created from the Medical Assistance Eligibility Result and can later be modified by caseworkers, who may add or remove only currently integrated-case members eligible in the latest CHIP decision. Reassessment covers ongoing eligibility and changes in circumstance, checking continued CHIP eligibility, premiums, co-pay limits, premium-payment rules, cancellation/re-enrollment waiting periods, and possible transition to regular Medical Assistance.

children.title: Reassessment
Ongoing Eligibility
children.summary: This section explains CHIP ongoing eligibility reassessment. It covers configurable reassessment frequency (default monthly), the rule that children must still meet CHIP eligibility criteria except open enrollment, and that premiums must be paid on time. It also notes state-specific cancellation and re-enrollment waiting periods, case closure after two consecutive unpaid months, and that premium status is supplied by an external vendor. It mentions caseworkers may also check regular Medical Assistance eligibility if CHIP becomes unavailable.

children.title: Change of Circumstance
Evidence Changes on Integrated Case
children.summary: This section explains how evidence changes on an integrated case trigger automatic reassessment across product deliveries, with special attention to CHIP cases. It lists changes that immediately end CHIP coverage, such as death, age/residency/citizenship/SSN loss, other comprehensive insurance, eligibility for regular medical assistance, or institutional/psychiatric facility residence. It also covers disenrollment effective dates, case closure versus removal of one child, and notes that income changes must be evaluated for eligibility impact.

children.title: Modification of the Benefit Group in CHIP
children.summary: Explains how CHIP benefit-group changes trigger automatic reassessment and updates to premium/copayment records. Covers adding eligible household members already on the integrated case (including newborns or children newly eligible due to circumstance changes), limited to open enrollment unless newborn or losing Medicaid. Added children get coverage from today for the remaining certification period at the household’s current premium plan level. Also covers removing a child, who stays covered through month-end; the case closes if no children remain, otherwise the child is removed from the benefit group.

children.title: Modification of Household Member Evidence on Integrated Case
children.summary: Explains how integrated case household member changes affect CHIP. Covers adding/removing members on the integrated case and benefit group, automatic reassessment across coverage types, eligibility checks, and when a child can be added (open enrollment, newborn, or loss of regular medical assistance). Notes effective dates, premium/copay updates, and that removed CHIP members stay covered through the last day of the month; cases may close if only one child remains. Pages 23–24 are Merative legal notices and licensing/trademark information.

Explains change-of-circumstance processing for CHIP integrated cases: evidence changes trigger automatic reassessment across coverage types, with manual review for possible regular medical assistance. Lists CHIP-ending changes (death, age/residency/citizenship/SSN loss, other insurance, institutional/psychiatric residence, voluntary stop) and disenrollment effective dates. Covers income changes, which generally wait until renewal. Describes benefit-group and household-member adds/removes, including newborns and open-enrollment limits, premium/copay updates, and month-end coverage for removed children. Pages 23–24 are Merative notices/trademark/legal text.

Explains CHIP reassessment. Covers ongoing eligibility checks (configurable, default monthly), continued CHIP rules, on-time premium payment, state-specific cancellation/re-enrollment waits, and possible regular Medical Assistance fallback. Describes change-of-circumstance processing for integrated cases: evidence changes trigger automatic reassessment, certain events end CHIP (death, age/residency/citizenship/SSN loss, other insurance, institutional/psychiatric residence, voluntary stop), and income changes usually wait until renewal. Also covers adding/removing CHIP household members and benefit group updates, including newborns and month-end coverage on removal.

Covers CHIP reassessment and when it is triggered. Explains ongoing eligibility checks (configurable, default monthly), premium payment enforcement, cancellation/re-enrollment waits, and possible fallback to regular Medical Assistance. Describes change-of-circumstance processing for integrated cases: evidence changes trigger automatic reassessment, certain events end CHIP, and income changes are usually deferred until renewal. Also details CHIP benefit group updates, including adding/removing members, newborn handling, and month-end coverage when a member is removed.

Business-level guide to Cúram Medical Assistance CHIP. Explains CHIP’s purpose, audience, and related documentation, then outlines the program overview: state/federal administration, benefits, premiums/copays, managed care vs fee-for-service, and CHIP’s place in the cascading MA eligibility hierarchy. Covers eligibility and product delivery creation, including open enrollment limits/exceptions, nonfinancial and CHIP-specific rules, financial/household rules, premium/vendor billing, and 12-month certification. Also summarizes reassessment, change-of-circumstance processing, benefit-group updates, newborn/add/remove member handling, and coverage end-date rules.
